title: Arrays and multidimensional arrays

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Arrays and multidimensional arrays; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. sources.locator: Learn Java; accessed 2026-07; Classes and Objects, Arrays, and String processing learning paths; atomic topic: Arrays and multidimensional arrays
sources.claim: A Java array is a fixed-length object with zero-based indexed elements, a runtime component type, and independently allocated rows for multidimensional array structures.

sources.locator: Java Language Specification, Java SE 21; Java SE 21; JLS §§6.3, 8.4, chapter 10, and §§15.12, 15.18.1: scope, methods, arrays, invocation, and strings; atomic topic: Arrays and multidimensional arrays
sources.claim: A Java array is a fixed-length object with zero-based indexed elements, a runtime component type, and independently allocated rows for multidimensional array structures.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A Java array is a fixed-length object with zero-based indexed elements, a runtime component type, and independently allocated rows for multidimensional array structures.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A sum routine handles an empty int array and traverses indices strictly below values.length without reading one element past the end. Your goal is to implement and test one- and two-dimensional array processing without bounds errors. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Loops and branching; pf abstraction calls. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: A Java array is a fixed-length object with zero-based indexed elements, a runtime component type, and independently allocated rows for multidimensional array structures. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Length cannot change, reference arrays start with null elements, covariance can fail at runtime, ragged rows have different lengths, and every access must satisfy its current row bound. The learner must reject this documented misconception: A two-dimensional Java array is one rectangular block, and every row always has the same length as the outer array.

includes: Create, index and traverse fixed-length arrays, including ragged two-dimensional structures.
Implement and test one- and two-dimensional array processing without bounds errors.
excludes: Length cannot change, reference arrays start with null elements, covariance can fail at runtime, ragged rows have different lengths, and every access must satisfy its current row bound.

outcomes: Implement and test one- and two-dimensional array processing without bounds errors.

examples.title: Arrays and multidimensional arrays: direct language case
examples.situation: A sum routine handles an empty int array and traverses indices strictly below values.length without reading one element past the end.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A sum routine handles an empty int array and traverses indices strictly below values.length without reading one element past the end.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Treat a multidimensional array as an array of row references, allocate and bound-check each row independently, and never infer column count from the outer length.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Treat a multidimensional array as an array of row references, allocate and bound-check each row independently, and never infer column count from the outer length.

examples.title: Arrays and multidimensional arrays: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A ragged int[][] traversal checks matrix[row].length for each row rather than assuming a rectangle from matrix.length.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A ragged int[][] traversal checks matrix[row].length for each row rather than assuming a rectangle from matrix.length.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Length cannot change, reference arrays start with null elements, covariance can fail at runtime, ragged rows have different lengths, and every access must satisfy its current row bound.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: A two-dimensional Java array is one rectangular block, and every row always has the same length as the outer array.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and test one- and two-dimensional array processing without bounds errors.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A two-dimensional Java array is one rectangular block, and every row always has the same length as the outer array.
misconceptions.correction: Treat a multidimensional array as an array of row references, allocate and bound-check each row independently, and never infer column count from the outer length.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: A two-dimensional Java array is one rectangular block, and every row always has the same length as the outer array.

analogy.example: a set of labeled workstations that accept copies of request values and process fixed trays or immutable text tickets can introduce Arrays and multidimensional arrays, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: reasoning about method contracts, scope, arrays, and strings
limitations: reference values can still alias mutable objects and Unicode or overload rules have no exact workstation equivalent
